content:
Dan Doel said:

That would be an erroneous answer to the question.

How about if you refrain from posting comments about constructive mathematics on threads where it's clearly off-topic, and I'll do the reverse?

Calm down, folks.

This is a thread about constructive mathematics, so jokes about constructive mathematics being "feeble" are probably unwise.
Sure, but likewise we also don't need comments about cardinalities not being a total order in the concurrent thread then.
Or about complete small categories not being partial orders in the other concurrent thread.
I agree with that, Reid. But I'm gonna move this little sub-conversation to its own thread, since Carette was in the middle of talking about something here.
This topic was moved here from #theory: category theory > -1-categories, constructively by John Baez
So, if anyone wants to talk about the etiquette of constructivism, here's a place to do it.

Reid correctly observed that if people are having a conversation based on classical logic, it's a bit annoying for constructivists to say that certain comments are wrong in constructive logic. It's sort of like you've got some people playing American-style football and then some British footballers come in and object to how they're holding the ball in their hands.
And conversely, calling constructive logic "feeble" in a conversation based on constructive logic is probably going to annoy people too.
Arguments about the relative merits of different forms of logic can be interesting (when polite and based on evidence), but sometimes they derail technical discussions.

I wonder if those disagreements can be resolved with Category Theory by realising that constructive and co-constructive logics can be tied together to form first order logic. :-)
I found lots of papers along those lines. A recent one is Pragmatic logics for hypotheses and evidence

Here is how I think the argument goes. As a Category Theorist you accept that a proof in a Category CCC is also an opposite proof in the category CopC^{op}Cop. So if a constructive proof is one that is provable in a Topoi, then any such proof is also one in the complement Topoi of co-constructive logics. And if it can be show that both of those together give first order logic, then there cannot be anything wrong with that either. Note that a lot of research recently seems to be focused on how one can avoid collapse into first order logic and have constructive and co-constructive logics work together. (I am not sure about this but I think I understood that when constructivists and co-constructivists agree - after a process of construction and attempted deconstruction - the result is a first order logic truth).
By the way, I think "co-constructive" logic would do well to call itself "instructive logic", since that's a way of spelling "nstructive" that seems rather good for advertising purposes.

Henry Story said:

Here is how I think the argument goes. As a Category Theorist you accept that a proof in a Category CCC is also an opposite proof in the category CopC^{op}Cop. So if a constructive proof is one that is provable in a Topoi, then any such proof is also one in the complement Topoi of co-constructive logics. And if it can be show that both of those together give first order logic, then there cannot be anything wrong with that either. Note that a lot of research recently seems to be focused on how one can avoid collapse into first order logic and have constructive and co-constructive logics work together. (I am not sure about this but I think I understood that when constructivists and co-constructivists agree - after a process of construction and attempted deconstruction - the result is a first order logic truth).

But Henry I don't think there is any evidence that "if it can be shown that both of those together give first order logic".
I also do not see any reason for co-constructive logic, except for an overzealous desire for symmetry. I love symmetry as much the next mathematician, but have not seen any good reason to do co-constructive logic, so far.

...and by the way a "complement topos" is not the opposite category of a topos, but it is precisely the same thing as a topos. I find it awful, but they use this expression just to emphasize that they employ the topos in question to interpret "coconstructive"/paraconsistent logic, and not intuitionistic logic.
It is a topos, viewed in a dual way. There are thus constructs which don't arise in a topos such as co-exponentials which I looked at about a year ago.

Valeria de Paiva said:

But Henry I don't think there is any evidence that "if it can be shown that both of those together give first order logic".
 I also do not see  any reason for co-constructive logic, except for an overzealous desire for symmetry. I love symmetry as much the next mathematician, but have not seen any good reason to do co-constructive logic, so far.

There are quite a lot of papers on the subject of complement topoi, and these are related to paraconsistent logics whose proponents I hear are known as "the brazilian school". One very intruiging relation is to the work of Popper https://twitter.com/bblfish/status/1248243867490672641

So here's an intriguing thesis: Where Intuitionistic Logic best describes Mathematical truths, it's dual, "falsification Logic" may be better adapted to scientific research. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11225-005-8474-7 https://twitter.com/bblfish/status/1248243867490672641/photo/1

- The 🐟‍‍ BabelFish (@bblfish)
James Trafford wrote some interesting texts on complement topoi such as Structuring Co-constructive Logic for Proofs and Refutations.
